name: Peñas Blancas - Los Reales Ascent
name_original: Subida a Peñas Blancas – circular desde Estepona
description: Cycle the challenging Peñas Blancas - Los Reales Ascent, a 22.0-mile route with 3339 feet of gain and panoramic Mediterranean views.
region_intro: Road cycling around Estepona offers a diverse range of landscapes, from the Mediterranean coastline to the Sierra Bermeja Mountains. The region features well-designed infrastructure, including dedicated bike lanes and coastal paths, providing varied terrain for road cyclists. Riders can experience relatively flat coastal routes or challenging mountainous ascents into the Andalusian interior. This area is characterized by its blend of sea views, rolling hills, and significant climbs. Best road cycling routes around Estepona The most popular road cycling route is Peñas Blancas - Los Reales Ascent, a 22.0 miles (35.4 km) trail that takes 2 hours 14 minutes to complete. This route features a challenging climb through mountainous terrain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is From Estepona to Casares – circular via EuroVelo 8, a difficult 28.7 miles (46.1 km) path. This route offers scenic Andalusian views and a mix of coastal and inland riding. Local road cyclists also love the Gaucín – View of Casares loop from Buenas Noches - Restaurante, a 65.6 miles (105.6 km) trail leading through the scenic Casares area, often completed in about 6 hours 24 minutes. Road cycling around Estepona is defined by its Mediterranean coastline, mountainous terrain, and scenic Andalusian landscapes. The network offers options for different ability levels, from coastal paths to challenging mountain climbs. The routes in Estepona are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.2 stars from over 50 reviews. More than 3,500 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Estepona's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Estepona?
A: Estepona offers a wide selection of road cycling routes, with over 40 distinct tours available. These routes cater to various skill levels, including easy coastal rides, moderate loops, and challenging mountain ascents.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Estepona?
A: Road cycling in Estepona features a diverse landscape. You can expect relatively flat coastal paths with stunning sea views, rolling hills, and challenging mountainous terrain as you venture into the Sierra Bermeja. This blend offers both scenic and physically demanding options.

Q: What do other cyclists say about road cycling in Estepona?
A: The komoot community highly rates road cycling in Estepona, with an average score of 4.2 stars from over 50 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, from coastal paths to challenging mountain climbs, and the beautiful Andalusian scenery.

Q: Are there easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Estepona?
A: Yes, Estepona offers easy and family-friendly options. The coastal paths, such as sections of the &lt;strong&gt;Senda Litoral&lt;/strong&gt;, provide picturesque and relatively flat rides with stunning sea views, passing by beaches, parks, and cafes. These are ideal for beginners and families.

Q: Where can I find challenging road cycling climbs in Estepona?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, the climb to &lt;strong&gt;Peñas Blancas&lt;/strong&gt; is highly recommended. It's considered one of the best cycling climbs on the Costa del Sol, known for its length and respectable average gradient. You can find this route here: &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e937245670/penas-blancas-los-reales-ascent" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peñas Blancas - Los Reales Ascent&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes around Estepona?
A: Yes, Estepona features several scenic circular routes. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e938820127/from-estepona-to-casares-circular-via-eurovelo-8" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;From Estepona to Casares – circular via EuroVelo 8&lt;/a&gt;, which offers stunning Andalusian views and combines coastal and inland riding. Another longer loop is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/17210706" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gaucín – View of Casares loop from Buenas Noches - Restaurante&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What are some scenic viewpoints or attractions I can see along the road cycling routes?
A: Many routes offer breathtaking views of the Mediterranean coastline, Gibraltar, and even Africa on clear days. You can also pass by historical sites and natural features. Consider routes that take you near the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2932134" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Estepona Old Town&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4775072" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Punta de la Doncella Lighthouse&lt;/a&gt;, or the dramatic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/395451" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guadalmina Gorge&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there routes that explore the natural beauty of the region, like canyons or mountains?
A: Absolutely. Beyond the coastal rides, you can explore routes that delve into the rugged Sierra Bermeja mountains. Some routes offer views of natural monuments like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4211862" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Manilva River Canyon&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4354990" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Canuto de la Utrera&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/21829260" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Padrón River Canyon loop&lt;/a&gt; is another option for exploring natural features.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Estepona?
A: Estepona's Mediterranean climate makes it suitable for road cycling for much of the year. Spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures, ideal for longer rides. Even winter can be a good time, with milder weather compared to many other regions, though it's always wise to check local forecasts.

Q: Are there any routes that pass through charming towns or villages?
A: Yes, several routes allow you to experience the local culture and charm of Andalusian villages. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e938820127/from-estepona-to-casares-circular-via-eurovelo-8" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;From Estepona to Casares – circular via EuroVelo 8&lt;/a&gt; route takes you towards Casares, a picturesque white village. You might also pass through areas near &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5247682" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Pedro de Alcántara&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Can I find routes with significant elevation gain for advanced training?
A: Definitely. Estepona is known for its challenging ascents into the mountains. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e937245670/penas-blancas-los-reales-ascent" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peñas Blancas - Los Reales Ascent&lt;/a&gt; offer over 1000 meters of elevation gain, providing an excellent workout for advanced cyclists. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/17210706" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gaucín – View of Casares loop&lt;/a&gt; also features substantial climbing.

Q: Are there road cycling routes that offer views of historical landmarks?
A: Yes, some routes will bring you close to historical sites. For example, you might encounter the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4404001" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Angel Aqueduct&lt;/a&gt; or the ancient &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4078869" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Hedionda Roman Baths&lt;/a&gt;, adding a cultural dimension to your ride.
tips: A well-known section among cyclists and fans of mountain passes. Road in good condition, although in the curves you have to be careful due to the lack of hard shoulders.

Puerto de Peñas Blancas well known among cyclists - on the road that leads here from Estepona there is a monument of two cyclists (a sculpture made by the artist Andrés Montesanto). The impressive views of the 990 m to the Mediterranean. Accessible throughout the year.

The old town of Estepona (Casco Antiguo) is a real highlight and offers a variety of places worth seeing. It captivates with its typical Andalusian charm, with whitewashed houses, flower-bedecked streets and cozy squares. The Plaza de las Flores is the heart of the old town. A picturesque square with colorful flowerbeds, cozy cafes and restaurants. Perfect for a break and to watch the hustle and bustle. 

The small alleys such as Calle Terraza or Calle Real are decorated with countless flowerpots. Each street has its own color palette, which makes exploring it a unique experience. Remember it!

faqs.question: Where does the Peñas Blancas - Los Reales Ascent route typically start, and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The ascent commonly starts from Estepona. You can find parking options within the town before beginning your ride. The route itself is a winding mountain road, and while traffic is generally light once you leave Estepona, hard shoulders might be limited in some curves.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: This route is accessible and enjoyable throughout the year, offering cyclists the chance to experience its challenges and beauty in various seasons.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required to cycle in the Los Reales de Sierra Bermeja Natural Park?
faqs.answer: There are no specific permits or entrance fees mentioned for cycling the Peñas Blancas - Los Reales Ascent within the Los Reales de Sierra Bermeja Natural Park. However, always respect local regulations and natural park guidelines.

faqs.question: Is the Peñas Blancas - Los Reales Ascent suitable for road bikes, and what is the terrain like?
faqs.answer: Yes, this ascent is specifically designed for road bikes. The road surface is generally in very good condition, being well-paved with asphalt for most of the route. While some sections after the Peñas Blancas pass might be less pristine, the final 2 km to the summit have been recently re-tarmacked and are in excellent shape.

faqs.question: What kind of views and natural features can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: You can expect truly spectacular panoramic views of the Mediterranean coastline and the Sierra Bermeja mountains. The route features several 'miradores' (viewpoints) perfect for pausing. The landscape includes pine forests, cork oak, juniper bushes, and unique abies pinsapo boiss trees. Don't miss the Puerto de Peñas Blancas Viewpoint.

faqs.question: What wildlife might I spot on the Peñas Blancas - Los Reales Ascent?
faqs.answer: Keep an eye out for diverse wildlife, including Griffon vultures, eagles, mountain goats, and roe deer, which inhabit the Mediterranean mountain environment along the route.

faqs.question: Are there any notable landmarks or points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, near the Peñas Blancas Ascent, you'll find a monument featuring two cyclists by artist Andrés Montesanto. Near the summit, the Refugio Salvador Guerrero offers a potential stop, even with a log fire during winter.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route also passes through sections of the [CIMA ML17] Peñas Blancas * Los Reales, Casares - Estepona, and Estepona - Marbella trails.

faqs.question: Is the Peñas Blancas - Los Reales Ascent route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: As a challenging road cycling ascent, this route is generally not suitable for dogs. It's best enjoyed by cyclists without canine companions.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this climb, and is it suitable for experienced cyclists?
faqs.answer: This climb is considered of "high difficulty" and requires "very good fitness," making it one of the most challenging routes in the region for road cyclists. It's highly recommended for experienced riders due to its average gradients of 6.2% to 6.4% and sections reaching up to 14.6%.

faqs.question: Is there an option to extend the ride beyond Peñas Blancas?
faqs.answer: Yes, for an even more demanding challenge and spectacular panoramic views, you can continue past the Peñas Blancas pass to the TV tower at Los Reales. This adds an additional 5.5 km with an average gradient of 7.7%.
